explanation: The statement says that whenever 'a prop_1' occurs, the system should perform 'prop_0'. This is typically understood as whenever prop_1 becomes true, prop_0 should also be true at that time or immediately after. In LTL, this is usually represented with the 'globally' (G) operator and an implication: globally, if prop_1 holds, then prop_0 must also hold at the same moment.
Whenever prop_1 holds, prop_0 must also hold. This is typically expressed as 'globally, if prop_1, then prop_0'.

Whenever prop_1 becomes true, prop_0 should also occur. This can be captured by stating that it is always the case that if prop_1 happens, then prop_0 must happen at the same time or immediately after (depending on the intended semantics). The most general translation is to require that always, if prop_1 then prop_0.

The requirement says that the autopilot must ensure prop_0 holds whenever prop_1 and prop_2 hold. In LTL, this is captured by stating that globally, if both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must also be true. This is written as G((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0).

This requirement says that when both prop_1 and prop_2 hold, then prop_0 must happen. In LTL, 'when A, B' typically means 'if A then (eventually) B'. Assuming that when both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 should eventually be true. This can be written as whenever prop_1 and prop_2 are true, prop_0 must (at the same moment or eventually) be true. The classic interpretation is: G((prop_1 & prop_2) -> F(prop_0)).
The requirement says that whenever 'prop_1' (apfail) is true, 'prop_0' must also be true at the same time. This is logically stated as: globally, if prop_1 then prop_0.

This requirement states that whenever 'prop_1' is true, 'prop_0' must occur (possibly at the same time or afterwards). In LTL, this is best captured by specifying that if 'prop_1' occurs, then eventually 'prop_0' must occur. This is expressed as 'G(prop_1 -> F(prop_0))', i.e., globally, whenever 'prop_1' holds, 'prop_0' must eventually hold.

The requirement specifies that whenever 'prop_1' (apfail) holds, 'prop_0' shall also hold at the same time. In LTL, this is captured by stating that it is always the case that if 'prop_1' holds, then 'prop_0' must also hold.
The requirement states that whenever prop_1 holds, prop_0 must happen. This is suitably captured by 'G (prop_1 -> prop_0)'. This means in all states where prop_1 is true, prop_0 must also be true.

Whenever prop_1 happens, the autopilot must ensure that prop_0 occurs (possibly at the same time or afterwards). This can be represented as: it is always the case that if prop_1 is true, then eventually prop_0 will be true.
